title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Impact of E-Sports on Traditional Athletics

texts.A: The inclusion of e-sports in mainstream multi-sport events is a transparent and ultimately desperate attempt to remain culturally relevant to a younger digital generation, but it fundamentally misinterprets what a 'sport' actually is at its philosophical core. Athletics has always been defined by the mastery of the human body and the constant testing of physical limits under conditions of genuine physiological stress and endurance. [37]Moving a thumb on a controller, regardless of the impressive reflexes or reaction speed involved, does not belong in the same competitive category as a marathon, a boxing match, or a competitive swim meet.[/37] The comparison simply lacks credibility. [40]By equating sedentary gaming with genuine physical exertion and athletic achievement, we are sending a profoundly dangerous public health message to a generation already facing a serious and growing obesity crisis.[/40] We should celebrate competitive gaming as a genuinely skilful hobby, but must never elevate it to the status of a physical sport.
texts.B: [39]The definition of sport has always been culturally fluid and historically contingent, constantly evolving alongside technological development and shifting social values.[/39] [37]Those who cling to 'physicality' as the sole and non-negotiable criterion for athletic legitimacy are wilfully ignoring the intense mental stamina, complex strategic depth, and extraordinary hand-eye coordination demanded at the highest professional levels of competitive gaming.[/37] Elite e-sports athletes undergo extraordinarily rigorous training regimes that often explicitly include dedicated physical fitness work designed to improve their cognitive performance and reaction times under competitive pressure. The remarkable commercial success of e-sports globally is not a cultural threat to traditional athletics; it is a compelling testament to an entirely new and legitimate form of human excellence that combines digital literacy with genuine competitive drive and mental toughness. To cavalierly dismiss this remarkable achievement as trivial is fundamentally to misunderstand what authentic competition truly means in the twenty-first century and beyond.
texts.C: [39]I agree that the 'physicality' argument deployed against e-sports is increasingly outdated and anachronistic in a world where chess and bridge are already formally recognized as legitimate sports by numerous Olympic committees.[/39] However, [38]the more fundamental issue with competitive gaming is the ownership of the playing field itself. In soccer, athletics, or swimming, no single private corporation owns the rules of the game or the equipment used. In gaming, the entire competitive ecosystem is controlled by a private company that can unilaterally alter the physics of the virtual world at any moment, for profit or any other commercial reason.[/38] This fundamental lack of competitive independence and genuine public governance is what truly separates organized competitive gaming from the democratic, internationally regulated tradition of global athletics and its established institutions. Without real structural independence from corporate ownership and commercial interests, competitive gaming simply cannot achieve the institutional legitimacy that genuine sport requires.
texts.D: The persistent and rather sterile debate over whether competitive gaming constitutes a 'real' sport is an unhelpful distraction from the genuine and significant value that e-sports brings: unprecedented global inclusivity and accessibility. Traditional sports routinely exclude those with physical disabilities, limited mobility, or those who lack access to expensive equipment, professional coaching, and adequate facilities. E-sports provides a truly global and broadly accessible competitive platform where the primary barrier to participation is simply a screen and a reliable internet connection. [38]While I fully share the concern that the industry is currently dominated by opaque and largely unaccountable corporate interests, I firmly believe this represents a regulatory and governance challenge that can be effectively addressed through determined and appropriate policy intervention.[/38] [40]The overall cultural impact of e-sports on global youth engagement, community building, and digital literacy is overwhelmingly positive and genuinely deserves recognition and meaningful support from established sporting bodies and governments alike.[/40]

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on the validity of e-sports as a competitive discipline?
questions.explanation: Expert A believes gaming does not belong in the same category as sports. Expert B argues that physicality is a narrow metric and that e-sports represent a 'new form of human excellence.'

questions.question: Which expert shares C's concern regarding the corporate control of e-sports?
questions.explanation: Expert C mentions the 'ownership of the field' by private companies. Expert D also acknowledges that the industry is 'dominated by corporate interests.'

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to B about the evolving definition of what constitutes a sport?
questions.explanation: Expert B says the definition is 'fluid.' Expert C agrees, noting that the 'physicality argument is increasingly outdated' and pointing to chess as an example.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from D on the social and health impact of promoting e-sports?
questions.explanation: Expert D sees the impact as 'overwhelmingly positive' due to inclusivity. Expert A sees it as 'dangerous' because it promotes a sedentary lifestyle during a health crisis.
